title: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
author: James Silberman
orientation: On March 9, 2021, Texas State Rep. Bryan Slaton filed HB3326, the Abolition of Abortion through Equal Protection for All Unborn Children Act, the third consecutive session a Texas abolition bill had been filed and the only bill answering the Republican Party of Texas' official 'Abolish Abortion' legislative priority. The article walks through the bill's eight stated purposes, its mechanism (repealing the PC 19.06 abortion exception), and the press release from Abolish Abortion Texas calling on Gov. Abbott and Lt. Gov. Patrick to publicly back it.
audiences: Texas voter weighing whether their 'pro-life' representative is actually voting for abolition
State legislator considering authoring or coauthoring an equal-protection bill
Pastor or theologian evaluating the bill's natural-law and biblical framing
Constitutional conservative interested in interposition and federal court defiance
Pro-life activist comparing incrementalism to immediate equal-protection abolition

questions.question: What exactly does HB3326 do, and how does it differ from a typical pro-life regulation?
questions.audience: Texas voter weighing whether their 'pro-life' representative is actually voting for abolition
questions.answer: HB3326 does not regulate abortion at all; it abolishes it by extending equal protection of Texas homicide law to every unborn child from fertilization. Mechanically, it repeals the carve-out in Penal Code 19.06 that currently exempts abortion from the murder statute, so that an unborn person receives the same legal protection any born person already has under Texas law.
questions.quote: HB 3326 simply repeals the exception for abortion in PC § 19.06, giving unborn persons in Texas the same legal protection as any born person.

questions.question: Why is the bill explicitly written to defy Roe v. Wade and Planned Parenthood v. Casey rather than try to work around them?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative interested in interposition and federal court defiance
questions.answer: The bill treats unconstitutional federal court rulings as void rather than binding, and asks Texas legislators to keep their oath to the U.S. Constitution by refusing to pretend the Constitution forbids the state from prohibiting homicide. The drafters frame Roe and its progeny as a 'legal fiction' that Texas has wrongly surrendered to, and they call on the state to reassert its reserved police powers.
questions.quote: as legislators, to fulfill our oaths of office to the United States Constitution, so help us God, by disavowing the legal fiction that the constitution prohibits this state from exercising its reserved police powers to prohibit and criminalize homicide and from exercising its constitutional and God-given duties to provide equal protection to all persons within its jurisdiction

questions.question: How does Rep. Slaton ground the bill theologically and philosophically?
questions.audience: Pastor or theologian evaluating the bill's natural-law and biblical framing
questions.answer: Slaton appeals to the Declaration of Independence's claim that governments exist primarily to secure God-given rights, with the right to life as the first among them. Any law or court decision contradicting that self-evident truth is, in his framing, not merely unconstitutional but contrary to natural law and must be rejected outright.
questions.quote: As the Declaration of Independence makes clear, governments are instituted primarily to secure God-given rights, the principal among them being the right to life. The right to life is the first among all natural rights, and must be protected to the utmost. Any law, rule, or court decision that is opposed to this self-evident truth is not only unconstitutional but contrary to natural law and must be rejected.

questions.question: Why does the article say HB3326 is the only bill that fulfills the Texas GOP's own legislative priority?
questions.audience: Pro-life activist comparing incrementalism to immediate equal-protection abolition
questions.answer: Texas abolitionists succeeded in 2020 in making 'Abolish Abortion' one of eight official priorities of the Republican Party of Texas, with explicit language requiring equal protection from the moment of fertilization. HB3326 is the only bill filed in the 87th legislature that actually does this, exposing every other 'pro-life' bill as falling short of the party's own platform.
questions.quote: This is the only bill currently filed in the 87th legislature which meets the "Abolition of Abortion" mandate of the [2020 Republican Party of Texas Legislative Priority]

questions.question: What rhetorical trap does the press release set for politicians who claim to believe an unborn child is a person?
questions.audience: State legislator considering authoring or coauthoring an equal-protection bill
questions.answer: The release argues equal protection is the logical corollary of personhood: if a fetus really is a person, the laws shielding born humans must equally shield those not yet born. Anyone refusing to support equal protection therefore reveals either that they don't actually believe the unborn are persons or that they don't actually want to follow the Constitution, leaving no honest middle ground.
questions.quote: If we truly believe that a fetus is a person, then the laws which protect born human beings must equally protect those not yet born. Those who oppose equal protection either do not believe that a fetus is a person or do not want to follow the Constitution.

questions.question: How does the bill use Greg Abbott's own past words to pressure him into supporting it?
questions.audience: Texas voter weighing whether their 'pro-life' representative is actually voting for abolition
questions.answer: In 2018 Gov. Abbott personally promised dying teenager Jeremiah Thomas that he would pursue outlawing abortion altogether in the next legislative session. The article frames HB3326 as the precise vehicle by which Abbott can finally honor that bedside promise, and publicly calls on him to do so.
questions.quote: ..its what we are going to be pursuing this next legislative session [2019] and that is to outlaw abortion altogether in the state of Texas.

questions.question: How significant is the body count the article cites, and how does it reframe abortion in Texas?
questions.audience: Pro-life activist comparing incrementalism to immediate equal-protection abolition
questions.answer: The press release points to over 55,000 babies killed by abortion every year in Texas, calling it a leading cause of death in the state. That framing rejects abortion as a political niche issue and treats it as the largest ongoing homicide crisis in Texas, demanding immediate and equal legal response rather than incremental regulation.
questions.quote: More than [55,000 babies] are murdered by abortion in our state every year, makingabortion a leading cause of death in our state. Thousands of innocent lives are ended unjustly every year without being provided the legal protection enjoyed by every born person in Texas.

questions.question: What does the bill's stated reliance on Justice Thomas signal about its legal strategy?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative interested in interposition and federal court defiance
questions.answer: By citing Justice Thomas's concurrence in June Medical Services v. Russo, the drafters anchor the bill in a sitting Supreme Court justice's own published view that the Constitution does not constrain states' authority over abortion. This positions HB3326 not as fringe nullification but as a state acting consistently with at least one current member of the Court's reading of the Constitution.
questions.quote: to follow Justice Thomas’s statement in *June Medical Services, L.L.C. v. Russo*, 140 S. Ct. 2103 (2020) that “[t]he constitution does not constrain the States’ ability to regulate or even prohibit abortion”

questions.question: How is the Texas Senate, and Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick specifically, being publicly challenged?
questions.audience: State legislator considering authoring or coauthoring an equal-protection bill
questions.answer: Lt. Gov. Patrick had just released his top 31 priorities for the 87th session, including two abortion-related items, but none of them abolish abortion. The release calls on Patrick to publicly support HB3326 and urges 'life-loving senators' to file a Senate companion bill so that the priority can move quickly through both chambers.
questions.quote: The Lt. Governor can further demonstrate his commitment to ending abortion in Texas by publicly supporting this bill. We also urge life-loving senators to file a companion bill in the Senate to speed the passage of this important priority.

related.title: Texas Abolitionists Make Abortion's Abolition an Official TX GOP Priority
related.reason: Directly cited in the article as the prior reporting on how 'Abolish Abortion' became one of the eight official Texas GOP legislative priorities that HB3326 is designed to fulfill.

related.title: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
related.reason: Covers the prior session's version of the same Texas abolition bill (under Rep. Tony Tinderholt) and its committee hearing — direct lineage of HB3326.

related.title: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
related.reason: The next chapter of this exact story: after the regular session ended without passage, Slaton refiled the bill in special session.

related.title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
related.reason: Shows the kind of 'pro-life' Texas Republican opposition that abolition bills like HB3326 have to overcome inside the legislature.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Provides the broader context on Texas pro-life organizations whose opposition to equal-protection abolition is the political backdrop for HB3326.

related.title: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
related.reason: Same 87th legislative session: contrasts the incrementalist Texas Heartbeat Bill that did pass with the abolitionist HB3326 that did not.

related.title: Immediate Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Lays out the underlying abolitionist case — also by James Silberman — for why immediate equal protection (the strategy of HB3326) is required rather than incremental restrictions.
